nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, heavily vegetated blackwater streams and tributaries with leaf litter and tannin-stained water

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flakes, nano pellets, and freeze-dried foods. Benefits from regular variety including frozen or live daphnia, cyclops, bloodworms, and brine shrimp. In the wild tends to consume insect larvae, small worms, and crustaceans.

summary: The Black Phantom Tetra is a generally hardy, peaceful species from South American blackwater streams, recognized by its iridescent black shoulder spot and tall dorsal fin. Males tend to display to one another with fin-spreading and deepened coloration — purely visual sparring. Well suited to community tanks.
careNotes: The Black Phantom Tetra is generally considered one of the easier tetras to keep. It does best in groups of six or more, which encourages natural shoaling behavior and brings out the characteristic male display: males deepen in color and spread their tall dorsal fins when facing one another, but this is purely visual sparring and does not typically result in injury. A well-planted aquarium with some open swimming space suits them well. Dark substrate and subdued lighting tend to bring out richer coloration. Note that Black Phantom Tetras are likely to consume dwarf shrimp and small fry if given the opportunity. The closely related Red Phantom Tetra (Hyphessobrycon sweglesi) is a distinct species sharing similar care requirements but with red and orange coloration rather than black.
breedingNotes: Breeding with intention is generally considered moderately difficult, though pairs or groups will scatter eggs spontaneously in a planted community tank — adults usually consume the eggs before they hatch. For deliberate breeding, condition adults separately with live or frozen foods, then move them to a dedicated spawning tank with slightly acidic water (pH around 5.5 to 6.5), temperature at the warmer end of the species' range, java moss or fine-leaved plants as a spawning substrate, and a sponge filter to protect the fry from being drawn into the intake. Females may deposit up to around 300 eggs. Adults should be removed after spawning. Fry initially require microscopic foods such as infusoria (single-celled organisms small enough for newly hatched fish to eat), graduating to baby brine shrimp after approximately two weeks.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.6 cm SL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (up to 18 dH), native range (upper Paraguay and Guaporé basins). SeriouslyFish returned HTTP 520 (Cloudflare block) and was unavailable; FishBase used as first secondary. Family confirmed as Characidae, Order Characiformes (FishBase extraction returned an artefact — taxonomy confirmed via iNaturalist/GBIF cross-check).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~1.75 in / ~4.4 cm), temperature (72–82°F / 22–28°C), pH (6–7.5), lifespan (~5 years), diet, and tank size (10 gal minimum cited — lower than other sources; 75 L used as cautious floor for a school of 6).
